answer: A FileHandle object will automatically close if it is not explicitly closed using the filehandle.close() method, but this behavior is unreliable and may not always close the file. Relying on this behavior can lead to memory leaks, and it is recommended to always explicitly close FileHandle objects to prevent this risk.
The callback-based versions of the node:fs module APIs are preferable over the use of the promise APIs when maximal performance (both in terms of execution time and memory allocation) is required.

filehandle.datasync() forces all currently queued I/O operations associated with the file to the operating system's synchronized I/O completion state, while filehandle.sync() requests that all data for the open file descriptor is flushed to the storage device. The key difference is that filehandle.sync() also flushes modified metadata, whereas filehandle.datasync() does not. You would use filehandle.datasync() when you need to ensure that file data is synchronized, but not metadata, whereas filehandle.sync() is used when both data and metadata need to be synchronized.

Read operations do not finish until data is available, which can prevent the process from exiting and the stream from closing naturally. To mitigate this issue, you can artificially mark the end-of-stream by pushing null to the stream, allowing it to close.

filehandle.pull() and filehandle.pullSync() are both used to read the contents of a file, but they differ in their approach to handling the data. 

filehandle.pull() is an asynchronous method that returns an async iterable, allowing you to read the file in chunks. It is suitable for scenarios where you need to handle large files or perform other tasks concurrently while reading the file.

filehandle.pullSync(), on the other hand, is a synchronous method that returns a sync iterable, which reads the file using synchronous I/O on the main thread. It is suitable for scenarios where you need to ensure that the file is read in a specific order or when working with small files.

In general, filehandle.pull() is preferred when working with large files or in performance-critical code, while filehandle.pullSync() can be used for simpler use cases or when synchronous behavior is required. However, filehandle.pullSync() requires the --experimental-stream-iter flag to be enabled.
